title: The 2022 Oscars: The Smack Heard ’Round the World
format: Sean and Joanna tape their reaction minutes after the 94th Academy Awards ends, covering the Will Smith and Chris Rock incident with an assist from Puck's Matt Belloni calling in from inside the Dolby, then CODA's Best Picture win and a telecast the hosts call the messiest Oscars they've ever seen.

segments.heading: The slap heard ’round the world
segments.note: with Puck's Matt Belloni, who was in the room

films.title: King Richard
films.note: The film Will Smith won Best Actor for, playing Richard Williams. His speech opened on Williams and family but pivoted, without saying so directly, to defending Jada Pinkett Smith: what Joanna calls a "halfway speech" that left both hosts unsettled rather than moved.

segments.heading: CODA wins Best Picture
segments.note: Sean called it weeks ago; Joanna is still not thrilled

films.title: CODA
films.note: Sean predicted this win outright weeks before the ceremony and is smug about it; Joanna spent the lead-up on Green Room laying out why she found the frontrunner status frustrating. Neither thinks it's the best film nominated, but neither thinks it's a disaster either.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: The film that lost. Jane Campion still won Best Director (the third woman ever, second in a row after Chloé Zhao) but was seated bafflingly far from the stage for a category she was a lock to win, unlike Will Smith's front-and-center table.

films.title: Drive My Car
films.note: One of several nominees Joanna wishes had actually been in the Best Picture conversation instead of the two-horse CODA-vs.-Power of the Dog framing.

films.title: Shakespeare in Love
films.note: Sean's prediction for how CODA's reputation ages: a movie that beat the presumed rightful winner, got trashed for years, then got reclaimed as genuinely good.

films.title: Saving Private Ryan
films.note: The film everyone wanted to win instead of Shakespeare in Love, the losing side of Sean's comp.

films.title: The Artist
films.note: Joanna's preferred comparison: a movie that made a lot of people happy, ran a clean campaign, and that nobody thinks about or holds a grudge over today.

films.title: Driving Miss Daisy
films.note: Cited as the genuinely embarrassing version of this outcome, the one CODA's win doesn't rise (or sink) to.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: The other genuinely embarrassing Best Picture winner in the same category as Driving Miss Daisy, per the hosts.

films.title: Do the Right Thing
films.note: Wasn't even nominated the year Driving Miss Daisy won, and it's still the reference point for how badly the Academy can get it wrong.

films.title: Ordinary People
films.note: Joanna's rebuttal to Sean's "will anyone rewatch this?" argument against CODA: small, quiet family dramas can and do stick around.

segments.heading: The telecast: tributes, bits, and musical numbers
segments.note: reunions, remixes, and a tired Bond franchise

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: One of Sean's favorite films of all time; its stars reunited on stage in a bit he found hokey, reducing what he calls an "incandescent, transformational" movie to nostalgia-baiting patter.

films.title: The Godfather
films.note: A 50th-anniversary reunion with Pacino, De Niro, and Coppola, oddly scored with Bad Boy Records music and cut short before any of them could speak. Sean thought Pacino and Coppola looked frail; Sean "Diddy" Combs got credit for smoothing things over afterward with a joke about settling the Will-and-Chris situation at the after-party.

films.title: No Time to Die
films.note: Won Best Original Song and got a 60th-anniversary Bond tribute the hosts didn't understand the need for, since the show did one in 2013 too.

films.title: Encanto
films.note: "We Don't Talk About Bruno" got an over-produced remix that stripped out the exact TikTok hooks that made the song a hit in the first place; "Dos Oruguitas" fared better.

films.title: Tick, Tick... Boom!
films.note: Lin-Manuel Miranda's wife testing positive for COVID scuttled what Sean imagines was a planned mini variety-hour built around this film. Andrew Garfield's Best Actor nomination for it also got Sean ribbed by texts from an absent Amanda Dobbins all night.

films.title: Cabaret
films.note: Invoked when Liza Minnelli, visibly unwell, was gently walked through a final award presentation by Lady Gaga. Sean's advice: turn off the podcast and go watch this instead.

films.title: Zack Snyder's Justice League
films.note: Won the fan-voted "Cheer Moment" for "the Flash enters the Speed Force," instantly memed and, per the hosts, the funniest outcome of a deeply strange night.

films.title: Army of the Dead
films.note: Cited as proof the "Snyder heads" were always going to mobilize hardest for any Twitter-voted category.

films.title: Minamata
films.note: The Johnny Depp film Joanna flags as the other fandom capable of hijacking a fan vote: she calls the Depp Army and Snyder heads the two most "bonkers, mobilized" fanbases on Twitter.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The Fan Favorite pick everyone actually expected and understood, unlike the Snyder and Depp surprises.

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: The one clip in the Cheer Moment montage the hosts approved of: Neo dodging bullets in the bullet-time sequence, which Sean calls an actual blockbuster-plus-craft moment worth cheering.

segments.heading: The moments that actually landed
segments.note: Troy Kotsur, Ariana DeBose, Jessica Chastain, and the also-rans

films.title: CODA
films.note: Troy Kotsur's Best Supporting Actor win, with Yoon Jung-shim staying on stage to sign for him instead of clearing the shot, was the moment that made Joanna cry.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Ariana DeBose's Best Supporting Actress speech, after a muted SAG Awards showing, brought the power both hosts had been waiting for.

films.title: The Eyes of Tammy Faye
films.note: Jessica Chastain's Best Actress win. Neither host is a big fan of the film, but both praise her campaign: consistently crediting below-the-line crew and visibly sitting through the Makeup and Hairstyling award.

films.title: Cruella
films.note: Costume designer Jenny Beavan's funny, rambling acceptance speech ("sometimes you need a movie like Cruella") kicks off a riff about comfort-movie logic.

films.title: Nightmare Alley
films.note: Folded into the same riff off Beavan's speech.

films.title: Four Good Days
films.note: Reba McEntire's nominated song came from this film. Sean initially assumed Mila Kunis was brought out as a Ukraine-adjacent presenter before realizing she's actually in the movie.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: Questlove's Best Documentary win nearly got swallowed by press-room reporters asking only about the slap. Sean hopes it doesn't overshadow the film, which he recommends.

films.title: Milk
films.note: The Oscar Sean Penn said he'd melt down over the Ukraine war, per an interview the hosts reference (not, they clarify, his Mystic River statuette).

films.title: Mystic River
films.note: The Sean Penn Oscar he was not threatening to melt.

referenced.group: The joke that started it all
Presenter mix-up, Rachel Zegler and Jacob Elordi

Summer of Soul.title: Summer of Soul (...Or, When the Revolution Could Not Be Televised)
